---
title: "Add a request web app to a data package's website"
description: >-
  How to use Propagate to add a web app for requesting subsets of a data
  package.
order: 1
---

::: callout-note
If you are using [Template Data
Package](https://template-data-package.seedcase-project.org/) as a template for
your data package, follow the [guide](TODO:add/link) on enabling the request
feature there instead. The template automates running Propagate, generating the
request web app, and inserting it into the data package website.
:::

## Building the web app

Use Propagate's `build` command to create a web app for a data package so that
people can request subsets of the data.

The `build` command takes two inputs:

1. The metadata file describing the data package.
2. The output folder where the files for the web app will be created.

### Building with default arguments

By default, `build` looks for a `datapackage.json` file in the current working
directory and creates the web app in `docs/requests/`.

To use the default arguments, open a terminal in the root of your data package
folder (i.e., the folder with your metadata file) and run:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate build
```

<!-- TODO: Add file tree of what the created files look like -->

### Building with custom arguments

To use a different metadata file or output directory, provide them as arguments:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate build path/to/metadata/file --output-dir path/to/output/dir
```

The only metadata file currently supported by Propagate is the
`datapackage.json` file described by the [Data Package](https://datapackage.org)
specification.

## Adding the web app to a Quarto website

If your data package is set up as a [Quarto](https://quarto.org/) website, you
can easily include the request web app by doing the following:

1. List the folder containing the web app as a `resource` in your `_quarto.yml`.
   This will make Quarto copy the folder into the final website assets. For
   example:

   ```yaml {filename="_quarto.yml"}
    project:
      resources:
        - docs/requests/
   ```

2. Add a navigation entry pointing to the web app folder in your `_quarto.yml`
   or link to it from another page on your website. For example:

   ```yaml {filename="_quarto.yml"}
    website:
      navbar:
        left:
          - text: "Request a data subset"
            href: docs/requests/
   ```

If you generated the web app in `docs/requests/` within your data package, it
will be available at `https://your-website.org/docs/requests/`.

::: callout-note
While we recommend using Quarto, the generated output is a self-contained static
web application that can be hosted on any service that can serve static files.
Simply make the generated output folder available as part of your website and
add a link to it so that Requesters can find it.
:::

## Publishing the website

<!-- TODO: Include section about running the Propagate command in GitHub workflow -->
